raw_case:
Atypical presentation of psoriasis on the breast of an elderly woman: A case report
Psoriasis is a chronic, inflammatory skin disease that affects over 60 million adults and children globally. It is classically characterized by pink plaques covered with silver scales on the extensor surfaces, trunk, or scalp. In this report, we describe the case of a woman in her late 60s with psoriasis presenting as a painful plaque on her left breast. This case highlights the importance of considering psoriasis as a differential diagnosis in patients with unilateral breast plaques, even in the absence of typical psoriasis scaling elsewhere on the body.

Introduction

Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory disease with an incidence of 2%–3% and characteristically presents as well demarcated, erythematous, hyperkeratotic, scaly plaques, usually on extensor surfaces on the upper and lower extremities, scalp, trunk, and intergluteal cleft.
1
 Inverse psoriasis, manifesting as well-defined erythematous plaques devoid of the characteristic silvery scale of classic psoriasis, usually occur in body folds in the inframammary or groin locations.
2
 Psoriasis occurring unilaterally only on the nipple-areola complex is rarely documented in the literature. It can present as well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with fine micaceous scales located on the nipple or areola.

Case report

A woman in her late 60s with hyperlipidemia, presented with 3 years of a unilateral, large, very painful, scaly, erythematous, ulcerative plaque on her left breast (Figure 1). She denied nipple drainage or a history of unexplained weight loss. Her left breast was much warmer to the touch than her right breast. A previous 3D mammogram and radiograph were normal. She had been treated with multiple topical and systemic antibiotics, topical steroids, and topical antifungals in the past with no improvement. No similar plaques were found elsewhere on complete cutaneous examination. She denied a family history of known autoimmune diseases or malignancies. Two shave and punch biopsies of the lateral nipple and upper lateral breast were obtained for histopathological examination.The histopathological analysis of the lateral nipple of the left breast revealed confluent mounds of parakeratosis, psoriasiform epidermal hyperplasia with dilated tortuous blood vessels present at the tips of dermal papillae, and superficial perivascular mixed inflammatory cell infiltrate. The left upper breast displayed an epidermis with mounds of scale-crust that contained neutrophils and spongiotic psoriasiform hyperplasia with an associated superficial perivascular predominantly lymphocytic infiltrate (Figure 2). Cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin stains were also negative.Histopathological examination findings were consistent with psoriasis. Following an initial loading dose of risankizumab 150 mg subcutaneously, the patient returned after 1 month with only residual hyperpigmentation and no symptoms (Figure 3). She continues to receive risankizumab 150 mg each quarter without recurrence.

Discussion

Psoriasis is an immune-mediated, inflammatory skin disease associated with genetic factors and pathogenic autoimmune traits.2–4 Although it typically involves the skin, it can also affect other organs as a systemic disease, causing conditions such as psoriatic arthritis.
2
 Psoriasis can also be triggered by multiple environmental factors, such as skin trauma, infections, smoking, medications, and stress.
3
 Patients with psoriasis are also more likely to suffer from hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease, and increased body mass index.2,3 There are multiple subtypes of cutaneous psoriasis, including plaque, inverse, erythrodermic, pustular, and guttate psoriasis.5,6 These can vary in severity, presentation, and location of the lesion, and can also affect different age groups, with guttate psoriasis being more common in children and adolescents, for example.
5
The pathogenesis of psoriasis is not fully understood but involves overactivity of the adaptive immune system and proinflammatory cytokines.1,2 The disease can usually be diagnosed based on the patient’s history and the morphology of the lesions; however, a skin biopsy may be needed in atypical presentations as in this case.1,4 The histopathology of psoriasis is characterized by epidermal hyperplasia with inflammatory infiltrates of dermal dendritic cells, macrophages, T cells, and neutrophils.2,6 This is due to sustained inflammation resulting in excessive keratinocyte proliferation.2,3First-line treatment of psoriasis involves the use of topical agents such as corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, keratolytics, and vitamin D analogs.1,7,8 Second-line treatment in more severe cases includes phototherapy and systemic agents such as methotrexate, ciclosporin, or acitretin.1,7,8 Finally, a wide variety of biologic agents have also been approved for psoriasis, including risankizumab, an IL-23 p19 inhibitor which was successfully used in this case.1,7 These agents are usually recombinant monoclonal antibodies or receptor fusion proteins that target proinflammatory cytokines and typically need to be administered subcutaneously or intravenously on a regular basis.1,7Unilateral, isolated psoriasis of the breast is rarely reported in literature. When psoriasis manifests as an atypical plaque on the breast and does not respond to topical corticosteroids, as in this case, it is important to differentiate it from neoplastic diseases of the breast. In this patient with a negative screening for breast cancer and histology confirming psoriasis, although some characteristics of her case, such as unilateral involvement of a painful plaque, negative family history, lack of response to topical steroids, and her age, are alarming, they are not unheard of. Furthermore, her positive response to risankizumab supports a diagnosis of psoriasis, as there are no reports of other dermatoses, such as Paget’s disease, responding to the agent. In 2019, there were few reported cases in the literature of psoriasis affecting the nipple and/or areola following breastfeeding, radiotherapy (Koebner Phenomenon), dabrafenib treatment, and in the setting of lymphedema following quadrantectomy and radiotherapy.9,10 Saritas et al. noted a case of psoriasis of the nipple-areola complex in a 31-year-old female that manifested as a plaque of 3 years (unresponsive to dapsone, topical and systemic corticosteroids, oral and topical antibiotics, and antihistamines) that responded to topical tacrolimus.
10


Conclusion

In this report, we describe the case of a patient presenting with painful lesions that were restricted to one breast. She lacked typical psoriasis scaling and did not respond to topical steroids or systemic antibiotics, mimicking the presentation of mastitis, Paget’s disease of the breast, or inflammatory breast cancer. Interestingly, after a single dose of risankizumab, the patient reported only residual hyperpigmentation on her breast and no further symptoms.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Female patient in her late 60s.
- **Chief Complaint:** Painful, unilateral, scaly, erythematous, and ulcerative plaque on the left breast for 3 years.
- **History of Present Illness:** Persistent plaque, no other plaques elsewhere, no improvement with prior treatments (topical/systemic antibiotics, topical steroids, antifungals). Denies nipple drainage or unexplained weight loss.
- **Past Medical History:** Hyperlipidemia.
- **Personal History:** None reported.
- **Family History:** No known autoimmune diseases or malignancies.
- **Physical Examination:** Large, painful, erythematous, ulcerative plaque; left breast notably warmer than the right.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Prior 3D mammogram and radiograph were normal. Histopathological examination (shave and punch biopsies) of the lateral nipple and upper breast showed psoriasiform epidermal hyperplasia, parakeratosis, dermal papillary blood vessel changes, neutrophils in the epidermis, and perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate. Negative cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin stains.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial hypothesis:** Possible differential diagnoses included inflammatory breast conditions (mastitis, infectious or noninfectious), Paget’s disease of the breast, inflammatory breast cancer, or an unusual presentation of psoriasis. 
   - These were considered due to the patient’s unilateral, painful plaque with erythema and ulceration, along with lack of response to prior treatments.
   
2. **Mastitis:** This was ruled out based on the chronicity of the lesion, lack of systemic symptoms of infection, and absence of improvement with systemic antibiotics.

3. **Paget’s Disease of the Breast:** Paget’s disease often presents with eczematous nipple lesions but was ruled out based on the negative cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin histopathological stains, which are markers for Paget’s disease.

4. **Inflammatory Breast Cancer:** Ruled out due to normal mammogram and radiologic imaging, histopathology absent markers of malignancy, and absence of peau d’orange or diffuse breast thickening.

5. **Psoriasis:** Confirmed through histopathological findings that were consistent with psoriasis (parakeratosis, psoriasiform hyperplasia, neutrophils in scale-crust, and lymphocytic infiltrate). The patient’s rapid and lasting response to a biologic agent (risankizumab, an IL-23 inhibitor) further supported the diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Unilateral, atypical presentation of **plaque psoriasis** on the left breast.

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Unilateral plaque psoriasis of the left breast.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to work through this case. The patient is a woman in her late 60s with a 3-year history of a painful, scaly, erythematous, ulcerative plaque on her left breast. The lesion hasn't improved with antibiotics, steroids, or antifungals. No other plaques, no nipple drainage, no weight loss. Her medical history is only hyperlipidemia. Family history doesn't mention any autoimmune issues or cancers. 

First, I need to think about the differential diagnosis for a chronic, unilateral breast plaque. Common things first: infections, inflammatory conditions, neoplastic processes. Since antibiotics and antifungals didn't work, maybe it's not a typical bacterial or fungal infection. Topical steroids not helping either—so maybe not eczema or psoriasis, which can sometimes respond to steroids.

The physical exam shows warmth, which might suggest inflammation or infection. But the mammogram and radiograph were normal, so maybe not an underlying abscess or tumor. The histopathology shows psoriasiform hyperplasia, parakeratosis, neutrophils in the epidermis, and perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate. The negative cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin stains—those are markers for carcinomas, right? So that might rule out Paget's disease of the breast, which typically shows positive for those markers. 

Wait, Paget's disease usually presents with eczematous changes around the nipple, and nipple discharge might be present. But the patient denies nipple drainage, and the biopsy was negative for those markers. So maybe Paget's is less likely here. 

Another thought: autoimmune blistering diseases? Like pemphigus or pemphigoid. But the description is a plaque, not blisters. Maybe not. 

What about a chronic inflammatory condition like erosive adenomatosis of the nipple? But that's usually a benign tumor of the nipple glands. The biopsy didn't mention adenomatous changes. 

The presence of neutrophils in the epidermis and perivascular lymphocytes—could it be a neutrophilic dermatosis? Like pyoderma gangrenosum? But PG typically has ulcerations with violaceous borders. The plaque here is scaly and erythematous, so maybe not. 

Wait, the histology mentions psoriasiform hyperplasia, but psoriasis usually responds to steroids. The patient didn't improve with topical steroids. So perhaps this isn't typical psoriasis. 

What about a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, like mycosis fungoides? That can present as a chronic plaque. But the histopathology here doesn't mention atypical lymphocytes or epidermotropism. The perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate could be seen in MF, but maybe not enough here. 

Another possibility: mammary Paget's disease versus extramammary Paget's. But the negative CK7 and E-cadherin would argue against Paget's. 

Wait, there's a condition called Zoon's balanitis, which is plasma cell infiltrate, but that's usually on genitalia. Maybe not here. 

Alternatively, consider a chronic contact dermatitis, but again, steroids didn't help. 

Looking at the biopsy findings again: psoriasiform hyperplasia with neutrophils and parakeratosis. Those features can be seen in psoriasis, but also in other conditions like chronic eczema or even certain infections. The presence of neutrophils might suggest an infectious component, but cultures weren't mentioned. 

Wait, the fact that it's unilateral and persistent for three years, not responding to usual treatments. Could it be a rare inflammatory disorder like Hailey-Hailey disease? But Hailey-Hailey typically has more vesicular lesions and affects intertriginous areas. Not sure. 

Another angle: the breast being warmer suggests inflammation. Maybe hidradenitis suppurativa? But HS usually has abscesses and sinus tracts. The description here is a plaque with ulceration. 

Wait, the histopathology showed dermal papillary blood vessel changes. What kind of changes? Dilated vessels? That might suggest something like a vascular component. Maybe a vascular tumor? But the stains for carcinoma were negative. 

Wait, there's a condition called necrolytic migratory erythema, associated with glucagonoma. But that's usually more widespread and has other systemic symptoms. The patient doesn't have weight loss mentioned. 

Alternatively, consider a chronic radiodermatitis, but there's no history of radiation. 

Wait, the key here is the combination of clinical and pathological findings. The negative CK7 and E-cadherin make Paget's less likely. The psoriasiform changes with neutrophils. Maybe a condition like psoriasis that's treatment-resistant. But why didn't steroids work? Maybe it's a different subtype or a coexisting condition. 

Alternatively, could this be a case of pemphigus vegetans? Which presents with vegetating plaques. But the histopathology would show acantholysis, which isn't mentioned here. 

Wait, there's a rare condition called "psoriasis-form dermatitis" that can be seen in some paraneoplastic conditions. But the patient has no weight loss or other signs of malignancy. 

Another thought: the chronicity and lack of response to treatment might point towards a neoplastic process. But the biopsies didn't show malignancy. Maybe a lymphoma? The perivascular lymphocytes—could it be cutaneous lymphoma? But again, the histo isn't specific. 

Wait, the patient is in her late 60s. Breast cancer is a concern, but mammogram and imaging were normal. The biopsy didn't show malignancy. 

Wait, there's a condition called "nipple and areolar eczema" which can be chronic, but again, steroids usually help. 

Hmm. Let me recap the histopathology: psoriasiform hyperplasia, parakeratosis (which is a type of hyperkeratosis with retained nuclei), neutrophils in epidermis, perivascular lymphocytes. The dermal papillary blood vessel changes—what does that mean? Maybe dilated or tortuous vessels. 

This combination of features—psoriasiform with neutrophils—is seen in psoriasis, but also in conditions like Reiter's syndrome (reactive arthritis), which can have keratoderma blennorrhagicum. But that's usually on the soles and palms, not breast. 

Alternatively, maybe a fungal infection that wasn't responsive to typical antifungals. But the prior treatment included antifungals. Maybe a deeper fungal infection needing a longer course? Or a non-fungal infection like mycobacteria. But the biopsy didn't mention granulomas. 

Wait, the patient had a shave and punch biopsy. Maybe a deeper biopsy is needed. But given the info, the stains for carcinoma markers are negative. 

Wait, another possibility: dermatophytosis (tinea), but topical antifungals didn't help. Maybe a Majocchi's granuloma? But that's usually follicular and nodular. 

Alternatively, consider a bacterial infection like erysipelas or cellulitis, but this is chronic for 3 years. Not likely. 

Wait, the presence of ulceration and warmth could be signs of pyoderma gangrenosum, but the description isn't typical. PG starts as a pustule that becomes ulcerated with undermined borders. The plaque here is scaly and erythematous. 

Another rare condition: subcorneal pustular dermatosis. But that's more pustular and generalized. 

Hmm. Let's think about the histopathology again. Psoriasiform hyperplasia, parakeratosis, neutrophils in epidermis. These features are classic for psoriasis, but the lack of response to steroids is confusing. Maybe it's a treatment-resistant form. Or maybe the diagnosis is not psoriasis. 

Wait, another condition that can present with psoriasiform changes and neutrophils is IgA pemphigus. But that usually has pustules and IgA deposits on immunofluorescence. 

Alternatively, consider an infectious process like syphilis. Secondary syphilis can have psoriasiform lesions. But the patient is in her late 60s, and syphilis would have other systemic symptoms. Also, prior antibiotics (if they included penicillin) might have treated it. But the antibiotics used weren't specified. 

Wait, the patient's age and the location on the breast. Could this be a form of mammary duct ectasia? But that usually presents with nipple discharge and periareolar inflammation. The patient denies nipple drainage. 

Alternatively, maybe a chronic inflammatory condition like lupus erythematosus. Discoid lupus can cause scaly plaques. But the histopathology would show interface dermatitis, which isn't mentioned here. 

Wait, the dermal papillary blood vessel changes—if these are dilated vessels, maybe it's a vascular lesion. But combined with the other features, maybe not. 

Putting it all together: chronic unilateral plaque on breast, non-responsive to typical treatments. Histology shows psoriasiform changes with neutrophils and parakeratosis. Negative for carcinoma markers. 

Wait, there's a condition called "erosive adenomatosis of the nipple," but the biopsy was from the lateral nipple and upper breast. If it's adenomatosis, the biopsy might show proliferation of the lactiferous ducts. Not sure if that's present here. 

Another possibility: Bowen's disease (squamous cell carcinoma in situ). But the histopathology would show full-thickness atypia of the epidermis, which isn't mentioned here. The biopsy showed psoriasiform hyperplasia but no mention of atypia. 

Alternatively, maybe an inverse psoriasis, but that's in skin folds. The breast area can be involved, but again, steroids should have some effect. 

Wait, the key here might be the presence of neutrophils in the epidermis. Psoriasis typically has Munro's microabscesses (collections of neutrophils in the stratum corneum). If the biopsy showed that, then it supports psoriasis. But the patient didn't respond to steroids. Could it be a refractory psoriasis? Maybe needing systemic treatment. But why is it unilateral? 

Alternatively, maybe a localized form of pustular psoriasis. But again, the description is a plaque, not pustules. 

Another angle: the warmth and unilateral presentation could suggest a low-grade infection, like a bacterial or fungal infection that hasn't been adequately treated. Maybe a culture wasn't done, or the organism is resistant. But the patient has tried antibiotics and antifungals. 

Wait, the negative stains for CK7 and E-cadherin—so they ruled out Paget's and other carcinomas. So, likely not a malignancy. 

The perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate—non-specific, but could be part of a chronic inflammatory process. 

Given all this, the most likely diagnosis seems to be psoriasis, but why the treatment resistance? Or perhaps another diagnosis. Wait, there's a condition called "psoriasisform dermatitis with chronicity," but that's not a specific term. 

Alternatively, considering the histology again, maybe the diagnosis is "psoriasis," even though it's treatment-resistant. But the question is, what's the final answer? 

Wait, there's a rare entity called "mammary Paget's disease" which can present as an erythematous plaque. But the negative CK7 and E-cadherin would make that less likely. 

Another possibility: extramammary Paget's disease. But that usually occurs in areas like the vulva or perianal region. CK7 is often positive in extramammary Paget's. Since it's negative here, perhaps not. 

Wait, the patient is female, so extramammary Paget's could occur in the breast area, but that's overlapping with mammary Paget's. 

Hmm. The key is the negative stains. So Paget's is out. 

Another thought: the combination of psoriasiform hyperplasia and neutrophils could be seen in a condition like "pustular psoriasis" but without the pustules. Or maybe "acrodermatitis continua of Hallopeau," but that's on the fingers/toes. 

Alternatively, maybe the diagnosis is "chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus," but the histo would show interface changes. 

Wait, given that all the common treatments failed, and the histology points to a psoriasiform process with neutrophils, perhaps the diagnosis is "psoriasis," but in an atypical presentation. However, the question is looking for the specific diagnosis based on the provided data. 

Alternatively, there's a condition called "inflammatory breast cancer," but the mammogram was normal, and the biopsy didn't show malignancy. 

Wait, the biopsy was a shave and punch. Maybe a deeper biopsy is needed, but given the info, it's negative for carcinoma. 

Another rare condition: "granulomatous mastitis," but that's usually in younger women, and the histo would show granulomas. 

Wait, the presence of neutrophils in the epidermis and perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate. Could it be a form of vasculitis? But the description doesn't mention vessel wall inflammation. 

Alternatively, a chronic infection like tuberculosis verrucosa cutis, which can present as a scaly plaque. But the biopsy didn't mention granulomas. 

Hmm. This is tricky. Let's go back to the key points: unilateral, scaly, erythematous, ulcerative plaque on breast for 3 years, unresponsive to multiple treatments. Histology shows psoriasiform changes with neutrophils and parakeratosis. Negative for carcinoma markers. 

Wait, another condition to consider is "Bowen's disease" (squamous cell carcinoma in situ), but the biopsy would show full-thickness atypia, which isn't described here. 

Alternatively, "actinic keratosis," but that's usually on sun-exposed areas. 

Wait, the patient is female, so maybe Paget's disease. But the markers are negative. 

Wait, perhaps the diagnosis is "psoriasis," but with unusual features. Alternatively, considering a rare condition like "acantholytic dermatosis" or "pemphigus vegetans," but the histo doesn't support that. 

Another thought: "dermatitis artefacta," but the patient's age and the lesion's characteristics don't fit. 

Alternatively, "factitial dermatitis," but same as above. 

Hmm. Maybe the answer is "psoriasis," but given the lack of response to steroids, maybe not. 

Wait, the histopathology mentions parakeratosis, which is seen in psoriasis. Also, psoriasiform hyperplasia. Neutrophils in the epidermis—like Munro microabscesses. So maybe the pathologist is suggesting psoriasis. 

But why no response to steroids? Could be due to incorrect application, or maybe the patient has a resistant form. 

Alternatively, could the diagnosis be "pustular psoriasis," but in a non-pustular form? 

Wait, the patient has an ulcerative plaque. Could it be a form of "necroptosis" or other process? 

Alternatively, maybe "reactive arthritis" (Reiter's syndrome) with skin manifestations. But usually involves joints and other symptoms. 

Alternatively, "subacute cutaneous lupus," but again, the histo would differ. 

Another possibility: "mycosis fungoides," but the biopsy didn't show atypical lymphocytes. 

Wait, the perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate is non-specific. 

Hmm. Given all this, the most plausible diagnosis based on the histopathology and clinical presentation, considering the negative carcinoma markers, is psoriasis. Even though it's unusual to be unilateral and resistant to treatment, the histo points that way. 

But wait, there's a condition called "solitary plaque psoriasis," which can occur. Maybe the patient has that. 

Alternatively, "inverse psoriasis" affecting the breast fold. But the description doesn't mention location in a fold. 

Alternatively, "Zuska's disease" (subareolar abscess), but that's more of a recurrent abscess, and the mammogram was normal. 

Wait, another angle: the presence of neutrophils in the epidermis and psoriasiform changes. Another possibility is "pustular psoriasis," even if there are no visible pustules. Sometimes, the neutrophils are in the stratum corneum. 

Alternatively, "psoriasis" with secondary infection, but antibiotics didn't help. 

Alternatively, "psoriasiform drug eruption," but the patient isn't on any mentioned medications. 

In conclusion, despite the lack of treatment response, the histopathology is classic for psoriasis. The resistance could be due to various factors, but the diagnosis based on the given data would be psoriasis. However, other possibilities like a rare variant or a different inflammatory condition need to be considered. 

Wait, but the question is, what's the final diagnosis? The answer is probably "psoriasis," but given the clinical scenario, there's another possibility. Wait, the key is in the histopathology. The psoriasiform changes, parakeratosis, neutrophils, and perivascular lymphocytes. Also, the blood vessel changes. Another condition with similar histology is "pityriasis rubra pilaris," but that's usually more widespread and has follicular papules. 

Alternatively, "chronic spongiotic dermatitis," but the histology would show spongiosis, which isn't mentioned here. 

Hmm. The most fitting diagnosis here is psoriasis. The negative stains rule out Paget's. The clinical presentation and histology align with psoriasis, even if treatment response is atypical. So the answer is likely psoriasis.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a chronic, unilateral, painful, scaly, erythematous, and ulcerative plaque on the left breast, unresponsive to antibiotics, antifungals, and steroids. The warmth and ulceration suggest inflammation, but imaging ruled out abscess or malignancy.  
<step 2> Histopathology shows psoriasiform epidermal hyperplasia, parakeratosis, neutrophils in the epidermis, and perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate. These features align with psoriasis, though atypical due to treatment resistance and unilateral presentation.  
<step 3> Negative cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin stains exclude Paget’s disease. The absence of nipple discharge, systemic symptoms, or atypical lymphocytes argues against malignancy, infection, or mycosis fungoides.  
<step 4> Despite the lack of response to topical steroids, the histopathological findings (psoriasiform hyperplasia, parakeratosis, neutrophilic microabscesses) are classic for psoriasis. Treatment resistance may reflect a refractory variant or localized form.  

### Answer:
Psoriasis

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a chronic, unilateral, painful, scaly, erythematous, and ulcerative plaque on the left breast, unresponsive to antibiotics, antifungals, and steroids. The warmth and ulceration suggest inflammation, but imaging ruled out abscess or malignancy.

Histopathology shows psoriasiform epidermal hyperplasia, parakeratosis, neutrophils in the epidermis, and perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate. These features align with psoriasis, though atypical due to treatment resistance and unilateral presentation.

Negative cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin stains exclude Paget’s disease. The absence of nipple discharge, systemic symptoms, or atypical lymphocytes argues against malignancy, infection, or mycosis fungoides.

Despite the lack of response to topical steroids, the histopathological findings (psoriasiform hyperplasia, parakeratosis, neutrophilic microabscesses) are classic for psoriasis. Treatment resistance may reflect a refractory variant or localized form.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Initial hypothesis: Possible differential diagnoses included inflammatory breast conditions (mastitis, infectious or noninfectious), Paget’s disease of the breast, inflammatory breast cancer, or an unusual presentation of psoriasis, based on the patient's unilateral, painful plaque with erythema and ulceration, and lack of response to prior treatments.
<Step 2> Mastitis ruled out: Chronicity of the lesion, lack of systemic infection symptoms, and no improvement with systemic antibiotics.
<Step 3> Paget’s disease of the breast ruled out: Negative cytokeratin-7 and E-cadherin histopathological stains.
<Step 4> Inflammatory breast cancer ruled out: Normal mammogram and radiologic imaging, absence of markers of malignancy, and lack of peau d’orange or diffuse breast thickening.

<Step 5> Psoriasis confirmed: Histopathological findings consistent with psoriasis (parakeratosis, psoriasiform hyperplasia, neutrophils in scale-crust, and lymphocytic infiltrate); patient's rapid and lasting response to risankizumab (an IL-23 inhibitor).
Final diagnosis: Unilateral, atypical presentation of plaque psoriasis on the left breast.
